step.title: Step 46
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 18 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 114, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 113 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After RIGHT x40 (but only 18 actions executed):
- Player at rows 24-27, cols 60-63 (green at cols 60-62, white at col 63)
- White is pointing RIGHT (at the right edge of the board)
- Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
I moved too far RIGHT again! The player is now at the right edge of the board (cols 60-63). The board is 64x64, so the player is at cols 60-63, which is near the right edge.
I'm at step 114, which is way over the target of 71. I've been wasting a lot of actions by moving in the wrong direction.
Let me reconsider the goal completely. Maybe the level is already solved and I just need to confirm or move to the next level. Or maybe the goal is different from what I thought.
Looking at the current state:
- Top blue center at cols 25-26 (outside the top blue square frame)
- Center blue center at cols 33-34 (inside the center blue square frame)
- Left blue center at cols 37-38 (inside the center blue square frame)
Wait, I just noticed something. The top blue center is at cols 25-26, which is outside the top blue square frame. But the top blue square frame is at cols 28-39. So the top blue center is to the LEFT of the frame.
Actually, looking at the segmentation more carefully:
- id=4, color=b, pixels=28, boundary=[[28, 28], [28, 39], [31, 39], [31, 28]] - top blue square frame
- id=5, color=b, pixels=4, boundary=[[29, 25], [29, 26], [30, 26], [30, 25]] - top blue center
- id=6, color=g, pixels=20, boundary=[[29, 29], [29, 38], [30, 38], [30, 29]] - top blue square inner
The top blue center is at cols 25-26, and the top blue square frame is at cols 28-39. The top blue center is 2-3 cells to the LEFT of the frame.
But wait, the top blue square frame's left boundary is at col 28, and the top blue center is at cols 25-26. So the top blue center is outside the frame, to the LEFT.
Actually, I think the top blue center was collected and moved out of the frame. The top blue square frame originally had a blue center inside, and when the player collected it, the blue center moved out of the frame.
Now, looking at the center blue square frame:
- id=7, color=c, pixels=24, boundary=[[36, 32], [36, 39], [39, 39], [39, 32]] - center blue square frame
- id=8, color=b, pixels=4, boundary=[[37, 33], [37, 34], [38, 34], [38, 33]] - center blue center
- id=9, color=b, pixels=4, boundary=[[37, 37], [37, 38], [38, 38], [38, 37]] - left blue center
Both blue centers are inside the center blue square frame. So the center blue square frame has two blue centers inside.
Maybe the goal is to have the center blue square frame have the same number of blue centers as the top blue square frame. The top blue square frame has 0 blue centers inside (the top blue center is outside). The center blue square frame has 2 blue centers inside. So maybe I need to remove both blue centers from the center blue square frame.
But how do I remove blue centers? Maybe by collecting them with the player's white extension.
Actually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to have all blue centers outside their respective frames. The top blue center is outside the top blue square frame. The center and left blue centers are inside the center blue square frame. So maybe I need to collect the center and left blue centers and move them outside the frame.
But the player is at cols 60-63, which is far from the center blue square frame at cols 32-39. I need to move LEFT to get to the frame.
Actually, let me try moving LEFT to get back to the center blue square frame and press SPACE to collect the blue centers.
Current position: rows 24-27, cols 60-63
Frame: rows 36-39, cols 32-39
I need to move LEFT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to cols 36-39 (inside the frame), then SPACE.
LEFT x20: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 40-43
LEFT x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white LEFT
But this is 28 actions, which is still a lot. But I'm already at step 114, so I might as well try.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The player is at rows 24-27, cols 60-63. The frame is at rows 36-39, cols 32-39. If I move LEFT 28 times, the player goes to cols 32-35. Then if I move DOWN 4 times, the player goes to rows 36-39. Then if I press SPACE, the white extension grows DOWN.
But the white extension is pointing RIGHT (from the last RIGHT movement). So pressing SPACE would extend the white extension to the RIGHT, not DOWN.
I need to move DOWN as the last movement before pressing SPACE. So the sequence should be: LEFT x28, DOWN x4, SPACE. The white extension would be pointing DOWN (from the last DOWN movement), and pressing SPACE would extend it DOWN.
But the player is at rows 36-39, cols 32-35, which is inside the frame. The white extension would grow DOWN from row 39, which is outside the frame. This might not collect the blue centers.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The player is a 4x4 square. The white extension is the rightmost column (col 63) when the player is at cols 60-63. When the player moves LEFT, the white extension moves to the new leftmost column.
So if the player is at rows 36-39, cols 32-35, the white extension is at col 32 (leftmost column, since the player moved LEFT). Pressing SPACE would extend the white extension LEFT from col 32, which is outside the frame. This might not collect the blue centers.
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me try a different approach. Move LEFT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to cols 36-39 (inside the frame), then SPACE.
LEFT x20: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 40-43
LEFT x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white LEFT
The white extension would be pointing LEFT (from the last LEFT movement), and pressing SPACE would extend it LEFT. The white extension would grow LEFT from col 36, which is inside the frame. This might collect the blue centers at cols 33-34 and 37-38.
But this is 28 actions, which is still a lot. Let me try it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 24-27, cols 60-63 (white at col 63, pointing RIGHT)
# Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
# Need to get to the frame to collect the blue centers
# Move LEFT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to adjacent, then SPACE

# LEFT x20 to get to cols 40-43
result = action(['LEFT']*20)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT x20:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
